verses.text: And David assembled all the princes of Israel, the princes of the tribes, and the captains of the companies that ministered to the king by course, and the captains over the thousands, and captains over the hundreds, and the stewards over all the substance and possession of the king, and of his sons, with the officers, and with the mighty men, and with all the valiant men, unto Jerusalem. Then David the king stood up upon his feet, and said, Hear me, my brethren, and my people: As for me, I had in mine heart to build an house of rest for the ark of the covenant of the LORD, and for the footstool of our God, and had made ready for the building: But God said unto me, Thou shalt not build an house for my name, because thou hast been a man of war, and hast shed blood. Howbeit the LORD God of Israel chose me before all the house of my father to be king over Israel for ever: for he hath chosen Judah to be the ruler; and of the house of Judah, the house of my father; and among the sons of my father he liked me to make me king over all Israel: And of all my sons, (for the LORD hath given me many sons,) he hath chosen Solomon my son to sit upon the throne of the kingdom of the LORD over Israel. And he said unto me, Solomon thy son, he shall build my house and my courts: for I have chosen him to be my son, and I will be his father. Moreover I will establish his kingdom for ever, if he be constant to do my commandments and my judgments, as at this day. Now therefore in the sight of all Israel the congregation of the LORD, and in the audience of our God, keep and seek for all the commandments of the LORD your God: that ye may possess this good land, and leave it for an inheritance for your children after you for ever. Guidance: David, near the end of his life, assembles all the leaders of Israel and stands to deliver his final great charge. He opens his heart: he had longed to build the house of God, 'and had made ready for the building,' but God forbade it because he had been a man of war and shed blood. Yet he traces the whole story to God's sovereign choice — God chose Judah, chose David's family, chose David, and now has chosen Solomon to build the house and sit on 'the throne of the kingdom of the LORD over Israel.' And David turns to the assembly with a solemn exhortation: 'keep and seek for all the commandments of the LORD your God.' The chapter is showing us a godly leader at the close of his life, humbly accepting God's no to his dearest desire, and pointing everyone to the sovereign grace and the commands of God. The chapter is teaching us to submit to God's appointments even when He denies us our cherished hopes; David wanted to build the temple, but accepted that the honour would go to his son, and threw himself into preparing for it rather than resenting the refusal. The chapter is teaching us that all our standing is owed to God's free choice — David repeats 'he chose... he chose... he hath chosen' — and that the throne is 'the throne of the kingdom of the LORD,' not man's; earthly authority is a stewardship under the true King. The chapter is teaching us the condition attached to blessing: 'I will establish his kingdom for ever, if he be constant to do my commandments' — God's promises, while sure, call His people to faithful obedience. And the charge to all Israel — 'keep and seek for all the commandments of the LORD your God, that ye may possess this good land, and leave it for an inheritance for your children' — teaches us that obedience is the path to lasting blessing for ourselves and our descendants. The chapter is teaching us to receive God's sovereign dealings with humility, to give Him the glory for every privilege, and to keep and seek His commandments, that we and our children after us may dwell securely in the inheritance He gives.

And thou, Solomon my son, know thou the God of thy father, and serve him with a perfect heart and with a willing mind: for the LORD searcheth all hearts, and understandeth all the imaginations of the thoughts: if thou seek him, he will be found of thee; but if thou forsake him, he will cast thee off for ever. Take heed now; for the LORD hath chosen thee to build an house for the sanctuary: be strong, and do it. Guidance: Here is the spiritual heart of the whole chapter — a father's dying charge to his son, and one of the great summaries of true religion in all of Scripture: 'And thou, Solomon my son, know thou the God of thy father, and serve him with a perfect heart and with a willing mind: for the LORD searcheth all hearts... if thou seek him, he will be found of thee; but if thou forsake him, he will cast thee off for ever.' The chapter is showing us what David most wanted for his son — not wealth, not power, not even the glory of building the temple, but to know God and serve Him with a whole heart. The chapter is teaching us the essence of godliness in this twofold call: 'know thou the God of thy father' — a personal, living knowledge of God, not mere inherited religion — and 'serve him with a perfect heart and with a willing mind' — wholehearted, glad, undivided devotion. To know God and to serve Him gladly: this is the whole duty and joy of man. The chapter is teaching us the searching truth that 'the LORD searcheth all hearts, and understandeth all the imaginations of the thoughts'; God is not deceived by outward show but sees the heart, so our service must be sincere, from the inside out. The chapter is teaching us the great promise and the great warning, set side by side: 'if thou seek him, he will be found of thee; but if thou forsake him, he will cast thee off for ever.' Here is the most encouraging promise — the seeking heart never seeks God in vain, for He is found of all who truly seek Him — and the most solemn warning — to forsake Him is to be cast off forever. The chapter is teaching us that everything hangs on this choice: to seek God or to forsake Him. And the charge ends with a call to action and courage: 'be strong, and do it.' To know God, serve Him wholly, seek Him diligently, and then to take courage and do the work He gives — this is the path David sets before his son and before us all. Let every reader take this charge to heart: know the God of your fathers, serve Him with a perfect heart and a willing mind, seek Him and you shall surely find Him.

The chapter is teaching us the searching truth that 'the LORD searcheth all hearts, and understandeth all the imaginations of the thoughts'; God is not deceived by outward show but sees the heart, so our service must be sincere, from the inside out. The chapter is teaching us the great promise and the great warning, set side by side: 'if thou seek him, he will be found of thee; but if thou forsake him, he will cast thee off for ever.' Here is the most encouraging promise — the seeking heart never seeks God in vain, for He is found of all who truly seek Him — and the most solemn warning — to forsake Him is to be cast off forever. The chapter is teaching us that everything hangs on this choice: to seek God or to forsake Him. And the charge ends with a call to action and courage: 'be strong, and do it.' To know God, serve Him wholly, seek Him diligently, and then to take courage and do the work He gives — this is the path David sets before his son and before us all. Let every reader take this charge to heart: know the God of your fathers, serve Him with a perfect heart and a willing mind, seek Him and you shall surely find Him.

Then David gave to Solomon his son the pattern of the porch, and of the houses thereof, and of the treasuries thereof, and of the upper chambers thereof, and of the inner parlours thereof, and of the place of the mercy seat, And the pattern of all that he had by the spirit, of the courts of the house of the LORD, and of all the chambers round about, of the treasuries of the house of God, and of the treasuries of the dedicated things: Also for the courses of the priests and the Levites, and for all the work of the service of the house of the LORD, and for all the vessels of service in the house of the LORD. He gave of gold by weight for things of gold, for all instruments of all manner of service; silver also for all instruments of silver by weight, for all instruments of every kind of service: Even the weight for the candlesticks of gold, and for their lamps of gold, by weight for every candlestick, and for the lamps thereof: and for the candlesticks of silver by weight, both for the candlestick, and also for the lamps thereof, according to the use of every candlestick. And by weight he gave gold for the tables of shewbread, for every table; and likewise silver for the tables of silver: Also pure gold for the fleshhooks, and the bowls, and the cups: and for the golden basons he gave gold by weight for every bason; and likewise silver by weight for every bason of silver: And for the altar of incense refined gold by weight; and gold for the pattern of the chariot of the cherubims, that spread out their wings, and covered the ark of the covenant of the LORD. All this, said David, the LORD made me understand in writing by his hand upon me, even all the works of this pattern. Guidance: David hands Solomon 'the pattern' of the temple — every detail of porch and chambers, courts and treasuries, the place of the mercy seat, the vessels, the candlesticks, the tables, the altar of incense, and the cherubim — with the gold and silver weighed out for each. And he reveals the source of this pattern: 'the pattern of all that he had by the spirit,' and again, 'All this, said David, the LORD made me understand in writing by his hand upon me, even all the works of this pattern.' The chapter is showing us that the temple was not designed by human imagination but according to a divinely-revealed plan, given to David by the Spirit of God — just as Moses was shown the pattern of the tabernacle on the mount and charged to make all things according to it (Exod 25:40). The chapter is teaching us the great principle that the worship of God is to be ordered by God's revelation, not man's invention; even the design of His house came 'by the spirit' and 'by his hand upon me.' We do not get to worship God however we please; He shows us the pattern, and we are to follow it. The chapter is teaching us the care and precision of God in the things of His worship — every vessel weighed, every chamber planned, nothing left to chance or whim; for our God is a God of order and beauty, and His house was to reflect His own perfection. The chapter is teaching us, in David's faithful transmission of the pattern to Solomon, the duty of passing on to the next generation not our own ideas but what God has revealed — David gave Solomon God's plan, not David's preferences. And the mercy seat and the cherubim covering the ark remind us that at the very heart of this divinely-patterned house was the place of atonement and the presence of God — pointing forward to Christ, in whom God and man meet, the true mercy seat (Rom 3:25). The chapter is teaching us to build our worship, our churches, and our lives according to the pattern God has revealed in His word, with reverent care, for His glory and according to His design.

The chapter is teaching us, in David's faithful transmission of the pattern to Solomon, the duty of passing on to the next generation not our own ideas but what God has revealed — David gave Solomon God's plan, not David's preferences. And the mercy seat and the cherubim covering the ark remind us that at the very heart of this divinely-patterned house was the place of atonement and the presence of God — pointing forward to Christ, in whom God and man meet, the true mercy seat (Rom 3:25). The chapter is teaching us to build our worship, our churches, and our lives according to the pattern God has revealed in His word, with reverent care, for His glory and according to His design.

And David said to Solomon his son, Be strong and of good courage, and do it: fear not, nor be dismayed: for the LORD God, even my God, will be with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee, until thou hast finished all the work for the service of the house of the LORD. And, behold, the courses of the priests and the Levites, even they shall be with thee for all the service of the house of God: and there shall be with thee for all manner of workmanship every willing skilful man, for any manner of service: also the princes and all the people will be wholly at thy commandment. Guidance: David ends his charge with one of the most strengthening promises in all of Scripture: 'Be strong and of good courage, and do it: fear not, nor be dismayed: for the LORD God, even my God, will be with thee; he will not fail thee, nor forsake thee, until thou hast finished all the work.' The great task of building the temple might well daunt a young king, but David points him to the all-sufficient remedy for fear — the faithful presence of God. The chapter is showing us a father's final, loving encouragement, grounding his son's courage not in Solomon's own ability but in God's unfailing companionship. The chapter is teaching us the cure for every fear and discouragement in the work of God: 'the LORD God, even my God, will be with thee.' We are not to be paralyzed by the greatness of the task or our own weakness, but to take courage from the presence of the God who goes with us. The chapter is teaching us the precious promise that God 'will not fail thee, nor forsake thee, until thou hast finished all the work' — the LORD does not begin a work in His servants and abandon it; He stays with them to the finish, as Paul would later write, 'he which hath begun a good work in you will perform it' (Phil 1:6). The God who calls to a task is faithful to see it through. The chapter is teaching us, too, that God provides not only His presence but also helpers for the work: the priests and Levites would be with Solomon, every willing skilful man, and all the people 'wholly at thy commandment.' When God calls us to a work, He furnishes both His own presence and the fellow-labourers we need. The chapter is teaching us to undertake the work God gives with courage, not fear; to lean on His faithful presence; to trust that He will not forsake us until the work is done; and to receive gladly the help He sends. 'Be strong and of good courage, and do it' — this is the word for every servant of God facing a great and daunting task, for the same faithful God who promised to be with Solomon is with all His people to the end.
